glossary.definition: Method of analyzing algorithm cost averaged over a sequence of operations, smoothing out costly outliers (e.g., dynamic array doubling gives O(1) amortized push).
Self-balancing BST where for every node the heights of the two child subtrees differ by at most 1; guarantees O(log n) search, insert, delete.
BST with one extra bit per node encoding color (red/black) and four structural invariants that guarantee O(log n) height.
Balanced search tree where each node can hold multiple keys and have multiple children; designed to minimize disk I/O, used in databases and file systems.
Two distinct keys mapping to the same bucket; resolved by chaining (linked lists) or open addressing (linear/quadratic probing, double hashing).
In a max-heap, every node's key is ≥ its children's keys; in a min-heap, ≤; guarantees O(1) peek at extremum.
Ratio of stored elements to hash table capacity (n/m); kept below a threshold (e.g., 0.75) to maintain O(1) expected operations.
Tree where each node represents a prefix of stored strings; supports O(L) lookup, insert, and prefix search for strings of length L.
Graph representation storing, for each vertex, a list of its neighbors; space O(V+E), preferred for sparse graphs.
Data structure tracking a partition of elements into disjoint sets; supports union and find operations, nearly O(1) amortized with union-by-rank + path compression.
Contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type. Random access O(1); insertion/deletion O(n) in the middle. Index from 0 or 1.
Sequence of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node. Insertion/deletion O(1) at head; search O(n). No random access.
LIFO (last-in, first-out) data structure. Operations: push (add top) and pop (remove top), both O(1). Used in DFS, expression evaluation, function calls.
FIFO (first-in, first-out) data structure. Enqueue at rear, dequeue from front, both O(1). Used in BFS, scheduling.
Tree where each node has at most 2 children (left, right). Height h; n nodes: h = O(log n) for balanced, O(n) worst. Traversal: in/pre/post-order.
Complete binary tree satisfying the heap property: max-heap has parent ≥ children; min-heap has parent ≤ children. Insert/extract O(log n). Used in priority queues.
Arranging elements in order. Key algorithms: merge sort O(n log n) stable; quicksort O(n log n) avg unstable; heapsort O(n log n); insertion sort O(n²).
Search in sorted array by comparing middle element: O(log n) time. Requires random access (array). Not suitable for linked lists.
A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function to compute an index into an array of slots; average O(1) lookup, insert, and delete, with O(n) worst case due to collisions.
An abstract data type mapping keys to values with insert, lookup, and delete operations; typically implemented by a hash table or a balanced search tree (e.g. Python's dict, a hash map).

canon.name: Dynamic array doubling
canon.form: Append is O(1) amortized: total cost of n appends = O(n) by geometric series 1+2+4+...+n < 2n

canon.name: BST search complexity
canon.form: O(h) where h is tree height; h = O(log n) for balanced trees, O(n) worst case for unbalanced

canon.name: Heap insert/delete-min
canon.form: O(log n) via sift-up / sift-down; build-heap from n elements = O(n) by summing height-weighted nodes

canon.name: Hash table expected operations
canon.form: With uniform hashing and load factor α < 1, expected search time = O(1 + α) = O(1)

canon.name: AVL rotation invariant
canon.form: Single or double rotation restores balance in O(1); height h ≤ 1.44 log2(n+2)

canon.name: Union-Find with path compression + union by rank
canon.form: m operations on n elements cost O(m · α(n)) total, where α is the inverse Ackermann function

subtopics: Graph algorithms (BFS, DFS, shortest paths, MST)
Complexity classes (P, NP, NP-hard, NP-complete, PSPACE)

glossary.definition: Class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
Class of decision problems for which a YES-certificate can be verified by a deterministic TM in polynomial time; equivalently, solvable by a nondeterministic TM in polynomial time.
A problem X is NP-complete if X ∈ NP and every problem in NP is polynomial-time reducible to X; intuitively, the hardest problems in NP.
A problem to which every NP problem reduces in polynomial time; need not itself be in NP (e.g., HALT is NP-hard but undecidable).
A transformation f computable in poly-time such that x ∈ A iff f(x) ∈ B; written A ≤_p B; used to show hardness.
Algorithm design paradigm breaking problems into overlapping subproblems, solving each once, and storing results (memoization or bottom-up table) to avoid redundant recomputation.
Algorithm that makes the locally optimal choice at each step; correct when a greedy-choice property and optimal substructure hold (proven e.g. by exchange argument).
Per-operation cost averaged over a sequence; potential method assigns a potential Φ to data structure states: amortized cost = actual cost + ΔΦ.
For a minimization problem, an algorithm has ratio ρ if it always returns a solution ≤ ρ · OPT; analogously for maximization.
Equation defining the running time T(n) recursively in terms of smaller inputs, e.g. T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n).
Class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic TM using polynomial space; P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE.
Class of problems whose complements are in NP; a problem is in co-NP if NO-instances have polynomial-time verifiable certificates.
Step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. Analyzed by time complexity (operations) and space complexity (memory). Must terminate and be correct.
Number of operations as a function of input size n. Big-O: worst case. Ω: best case. Θ: tight bound. Common: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2^n).
Function that calls itself with a smaller sub-problem, with a base case to terminate. Time often T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) (master theorem).
BFS: uses queue, finds shortest path (unweighted). DFS: uses stack/recursion, detects cycles, topological sort. Both O(V+E).

canon.name: Master theorem
canon.form: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): (1) if f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)) then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)); (2) if f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) log^k n) then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) log^(k+1) n); (3) if f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)) and regularity holds then T(n) = Θ(f(n))

canon.name: Comparison-sort lower bound
canon.form: Any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case (decision-tree argument: n! leaves, height ≥ log2(n!)  = Ω(n log n))

canon.name: Dijkstra's algorithm
canon.form: Single-source shortest paths for non-negative weights; O((V+E) log V) with binary heap, O(V log V + E) with Fibonacci heap

canon.name: Bellman-Ford algorithm
canon.form: Single-source shortest paths allowing negative weights; O(VE); detects negative cycles; relaxes all edges V-1 times

canon.name: Floyd-Warshall algorithm
canon.form: All-pairs shortest paths; O(V^3) DP: d[i][j][k] = min(d[i][j][k-1], d[i][k][k-1] + d[k][j][k-1])

canon.name: Kruskal's MST
canon.form: Sort edges by weight, add edge if it doesn't form a cycle (union-find); O(E log E); correctness by cut property

canon.name: Prim's MST
canon.form: Grow MST greedily from a root, always adding minimum-weight crossing edge; O(E log V) with binary heap

canon.name: Cook-Levin theorem
canon.form: SAT is NP-complete: every NP problem reduces to SAT in polynomial time; proof encodes TM computation as Boolean formula

canon.name: Mergesort
canon.form: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n) → T(n) = Θ(n log n); stable; O(n) extra space

canon.name: Quicksort
canon.form: Expected O(n log n) with random pivot; worst-case O(n^2); in-place (O(log n) stack space); not stable

canon.name: Radix/Counting sort
canon.form: O(n + k) time for integers in [0,k]; counting sort: count frequencies, compute prefix sums, place; radix sort: d passes of counting sort on each digit → O(d(n+k))

canon.name: Karatsuba multiplication
canon.form: Multiply two n-digit numbers in O(n^(log_2 3)) ≈ O(n^1.585) using 3 subproblems of size n/2 instead of 4

canon.name: Max-flow min-cut theorem
canon.form: In any flow network, max flow = min cut capacity; Ford-Fulkerson finds max flow in O(E · |f*|); Edmonds-Karp in O(VE^2)

subtopics: Finite automata (DFA, NFA) and regular languages

glossary.definition: 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F): states Q, alphabet Σ, transition function δ: Q×Σ→Q, start state q0, accept states F; recognizes exactly the regular languages.
Like a DFA but δ: Q×Σ_ε → P(Q); accepts if any computation path reaches an accept state; equivalent in power to DFA (subset construction converts).
A language recognized by some DFA; equivalently described by a regular expression; closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, intersection.
For any regular language L, ∃ pumping length p such that any string s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p can be split s=xyz with |xy|≤p, |y|≥1, and xy^iz ∈ L for all i≥0; used to prove non-regularity.
4-tuple (V, Σ, R, S): variables V, terminals Σ, production rules R of form A→α, start variable S; generates context-free languages.
NFA augmented with a stack; recognizes exactly the context-free languages; can be nondeterministic.
For any CFL L, ∃ p such that any s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p can be split s=uvxyz with |vy|≥1, |vxy|≤p, and uv^ixy^iz ∈ L for all i≥0; used to prove non-context-freeness.
7-tuple (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, q_accept, q_reject) operating on an infinite tape; the canonical model of computation; computes exactly the recursively enumerable (RE) languages.
A language for which there exists a TM that always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input; also called recursive.
A language for which some TM halts and accepts every string in the language, but may loop on strings not in it; also called recursively enumerable (RE).
HALT_TM = {⟨M,w⟩ | TM M halts on input w}; undecidable (proven by diagonalization); RE but not co-RE.
A mapping reduction A ≤_m B: a computable function f where x∈A iff f(x)∈B; if B is decidable then so is A; contrapositively, if A is undecidable then B is undecidable.
Any nontrivial semantic property of Turing machine languages is undecidable; i.e., no algorithm can decide whether the language of an arbitrary TM has a nontrivial property.
An abstract computational model with an infinite tape, read/write head, and state transition table; defines the limits of computability (the Church-Turing thesis).
An abstract state machine (finite automaton, pushdown automaton, Turing machine) that processes input strings; used to model computations and define language classes.
A computational model with finitely many states that reads input symbols and transitions between states; accepts a regular language; two types: DFA (deterministic) and NFA (nondeterministic).

canon.name: Subset construction (NFA→DFA)
canon.form: States of DFA = subsets of NFA states; at most 2^n states; construction runs in O(2^n · |Σ|) time

canon.name: Myhill-Nerode theorem
canon.definition: A language L is regular iff the equivalence relation x ≡_L y (∀z: xz∈L↔yz∈L) has finitely many classes; the minimum DFA has exactly as many states as equivalence classes
canon.form: L regular ↔ index(≡_L) finite; min-DFA states = index(≡_L)

canon.name: Church-Turing thesis
canon.form: Every effectively computable function is computable by a Turing machine; not provable, but universally accepted as a definition of computability

canon.name: Diagonalization / Halting undecidability
canon.form: Assume TM H decides HALT_TM; construct D: on input ⟨M⟩, run H on ⟨M,⟨M⟩⟩, do the opposite → contradiction on D(⟨D⟩)

canon.name: Chomsky hierarchy
canon.form: Type 0 (RE, TM) ⊃ Type 1 (context-sensitive, LBA) ⊃ Type 2 (CFL, PDA) ⊃ Type 3 (regular, DFA)

canon.name: CYK algorithm
canon.form: Decides membership in CFL from CNF grammar in O(n^3 · |G|) time using DP table T[i][j][A] = true if A derives s[i..j]

glossary.definition: A propositional formula that is true under every truth assignment; e.g., p ∨ ¬p.
A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one truth assignment making it true; SAT is the canonical NP-complete problem.
Proof technique: establish base case P(0), then show P(k) → P(k+1) for all k ≥ 0; concludes P(n) for all n ≥ 0.
Assume P(0), P(1), ..., P(k) all hold, then prove P(k+1); equivalent in power to ordinary induction.
A function that is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto); establishes that two sets have the same cardinality.
Proof that the reals are uncountable: for any enumeration of reals, construct a real differing from the n-th real in the n-th decimal place.
Arithmetic on residues mod n; a ≡ b (mod n) iff n | (a-b); forms the ring Z/nZ.
Count of integers in [1,n] coprime to n; φ(p) = p-1 for prime p; φ(mn) = φ(m)φ(n) if gcd(m,n)=1.
If n+1 objects are placed into n boxes, at least one box contains ≥ 2 objects; generalized: if kn+1 objects in n boxes, some box has ≥ k+1.
Graphs G and H are isomorphic if there is a bijection f: V(G)→V(H) preserving adjacency; not known to be in P or NP-complete.
Path/circuit visiting every edge exactly once; exists in undirected graph iff 0 (path) or 0 vertices (circuit) have odd degree.
Graph embeddable in the plane without edge crossings; by Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 for connected planar graphs.
Set of vertices V and edges E. Directed (digraph) or undirected. Weighted or unweighted. Representations: adjacency matrix O(V²) or adjacency list O(V+E).
Connected acyclic undirected graph. n vertices, n-1 edges. Rooted tree has a designated root; leaves have no children.
Base case + inductive step: assume P(k) true, prove P(k+1). Strong induction: assume all P(j) for j ≤ k. Used to prove properties of integers.
Counting principles: permutations P(n,k) = n!/(n-k)!; combinations C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!); binomial theorem (a+b)^n = Σ C(n,k) a^(n-k) b^k.
A statement that is definitely either true or false (never both); the atomic unit of propositional logic, combined via connectives such as AND, OR, NOT, and implication.
In propositional logic, a propositional variable or its negation (e.g. x or NOT x); the building block of clauses in conjunctive normal form and of SAT instances.
A binary relation on a set A is a subset R of A x A; characterized by properties like reflexivity, symmetry, antisymmetry, and transitivity (e.g. an equivalence relation or a partial order).
In a graph, a walk that visits no vertex more than once: a sequence of distinct vertices each adjacent to the next.
In a graph, a closed walk of length at least three whose vertices are distinct except that the last equals the first; a graph with no cycles is acyclic (a forest).
An equation defining each term of a sequence as a function of preceding terms (e.g. Fibonacci a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2}); solved in closed form via characteristic equations or generating functions.
An equation defining each term of a sequence as a function of preceding terms (e.g. Fibonacci a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2}); solved in closed form via characteristic equations or generating functions.
A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one truth assignment making it true; SAT is the canonical NP-complete problem.
Proof technique: establish base case P(0), then show P(k) → P(k+1) for all k ≥ 0; concludes P(n) for all n ≥ 0.
Assume P(0), P(1), ..., P(k) all hold, then prove P(k+1); equivalent in power to ordinary induction.
A function that is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto); establishes that two sets have the same cardinality.
Proof that the reals are uncountable: for any enumeration of reals, construct a real differing from the n-th real in the n-th decimal place.
Arithmetic on residues mod n; a ≡ b (mod n) iff n | (a-b); forms the ring Z/nZ.
Count of integers in [1,n] coprime to n; φ(p) = p-1 for prime p; φ(mn) = φ(m)φ(n) if gcd(m,n)=1.
If n+1 objects are placed into n boxes, at least one box contains ≥ 2 objects; generalized: if kn+1 objects in n boxes, some box has ≥ k+1.
Graphs G and H are isomorphic if there is a bijection f: V(G)→V(H) preserving adjacency; not known to be in P or NP-complete.
Path/circuit visiting every edge exactly once; exists in undirected graph iff 0 (path) or 0 vertices (circuit) have odd degree.
Graph embeddable in the plane without edge crossings; by Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 for connected planar graphs.

canon.name: Binomial theorem
canon.form: (x+y)^n = Σ_{k=0}^{n} C(n,k) x^k y^(n-k)

canon.name: Fermat's little theorem
canon.form: For prime p and a not divisible by p: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p); equivalently a^p ≡ a (mod p)

canon.name: Euler's theorem
canon.form: For gcd(a,n)=1: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n); generalizes Fermat's little theorem

canon.name: Euler's formula (planar graphs)
canon.form: V - E + F = 2 for any connected planar graph; corollary: E ≤ 3V - 6 for simple planar graphs (V ≥ 3)

canon.name: De Morgan's laws
canon.form: ¬(P ∧ Q) ≡ ¬P ∨ ¬Q; ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q; dual forms for sets: (A ∩ B)^c = A^c ∪ B^c

canon.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
canon.form: If n_1,...,n_k are pairwise coprime, then for any a_1,...,a_k there exists a unique x mod (n_1...n_k) with x ≡ a_i (mod n_i)

glossary.definition: A program in execution; has its own address space, PC, registers, open files, and OS state; isolated from other processes.
Lightweight execution unit within a process; shares address space and file descriptors with other threads of the same process; has its own stack and registers.
OS operation saving the CPU state of a running process/thread and loading the state of another; overhead measured in microseconds.
Synchronization primitive with integer value; P (wait/down) decrements and blocks if <0; V (signal/up) increments and wakes a blocked process; invented by Dijkstra.
Binary semaphore used to ensure only one thread executes a critical section at a time; provides ownership semantics.
Circular wait among processes where each holds a resource another needs; necessary conditions (Coffman): mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait.
Deadlock avoidance algorithm by Dijkstra; grants resource requests only if remaining allocation leaves the system in a safe state (where all processes can finish sequentially).
Abstraction giving each process a private large address space implemented by mapping virtual pages to physical frames; enables demand paging and isolation.
Trap raised when a process accesses a virtual page not currently in physical memory; OS handles by loading the page from disk (demand paging).
Hardware cache for recent virtual-to-physical address translations; hit avoids page table walk; managed by OS on a TLB miss (software-managed) or hardware.
Unix file system data structure storing file metadata (permissions, timestamps, size, pointers to data blocks) but not the filename.
CPU scheduling where each process gets a fixed time quantum in circular order; preemptive; fair but causes many context switches if quantum is too small.
System software that manages hardware resources (CPU, memory, I/O) and provides a runtime environment for applications; key abstractions: processes, virtual memory, file system.

canon.name: Peterson's algorithm (2-process mutex)
canon.form: Uses flag[i] and turn variables; satisfies mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting for 2 processes on a sequentially consistent memory model

canon.name: Producer-consumer with semaphores
canon.form: full=0, empty=N, mutex=1; producer: P(empty),P(mutex),add,V(mutex),V(full); consumer: P(full),P(mutex),remove,V(mutex),V(empty)

canon.name: Dining philosophers (monitor solution)
canon.form: Each philosopher picks up both forks atomically inside a monitor; condition variables used to wait when a fork is unavailable; avoids deadlock

canon.name: LRU page replacement
canon.form: Evict the page whose most recent use was furthest in the past; optimal approximation; O(1) per access using doubly-linked list + hash map

canon.name: Optimal page replacement (Belady)
canon.form: Evict page not used for the longest time in the future; achieves minimum page faults; impractical online, used as benchmark

canon.name: Coffman conditions for deadlock
canon.form: Deadlock requires ALL of: (1) mutual exclusion, (2) hold-and-wait, (3) no preemption, (4) circular wait; remove any one to prevent deadlock

glossary.definition: 7-layer reference model: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application; each layer provides services to the one above.
Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered byte-stream transport protocol; uses three-way handshake, sequence numbers, ACKs, and flow/congestion control.
Connectionless, unreliable transport protocol with minimal overhead; no handshake, ordering, or retransmission; used for DNS, video streaming, gaming.
32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) network-layer identifier for a host or interface; hierarchically structured to enable routing.
IP address allocation using variable-length subnet masks written as a.b.c.d/prefix; aggregates routes and reduces routing table size.
Path-vector routing protocol used between autonomous systems on the Internet; makes routing decisions based on paths, network policies, and rule sets.
Link-state intra-domain routing protocol; each router floods its link-state advertisements; runs Dijkstra to compute shortest paths.
Connection establishment: client sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK, client sends ACK; establishes sequence numbers for reliable delivery.
TCP sender-side limit on unacknowledged bytes; increased in slow start (exponentially) and congestion avoidance (linearly); halved on packet loss.
Hierarchical distributed database mapping hostnames to IP addresses; resolvers query root, TLD, and authoritative name servers; uses UDP port 53.
Cryptographic protocol providing authentication, confidentiality, and integrity over TCP; uses asymmetric crypto for key exchange, symmetric for bulk encryption.
Maps multiple private IP addresses to a single public IP using port numbers; breaks end-to-end connectivity; ubiquitous in IPv4 networks.
A collection of interconnected computers or devices that communicate by exchanging data according to shared protocols; characterized by topology, bandwidth, latency, and protocol stack.
A set of rules governing the format and timing of messages exchanged between communicating entities; e.g., TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS. Protocols are layered in the OSI or TCP/IP model.
The transformation of data using a key and cipher so that only authorized parties can recover the original (plaintext) from the ciphertext; fundamental to network security (TLS, HTTPS).

canon.name: Shannon capacity
canon.form: C = B log2(1 + S/N) bits/second; C = channel capacity, B = bandwidth (Hz), S/N = signal-to-noise ratio

canon.name: Nyquist theorem
canon.form: Maximum data rate = 2B log2(V) bits/s for noiseless channel with bandwidth B and V discrete signal levels

canon.name: TCP slow start
canon.form: cwnd starts at 1 MSS; doubles each RTT until ssthresh; then enters congestion avoidance (additive increase); on loss: ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = 1 (Tahoe) or cwnd = ssthresh (Reno)

canon.name: Dijkstra link-state routing
canon.form: Each router runs Dijkstra on full topology graph; O((V+E) log V); requires global topology knowledge via link-state flooding

canon.name: Distance-vector routing (Bellman-Ford)
canon.form: Each router maintains distance estimates to all destinations; exchanges with neighbors; d_x(y) = min_v{c(x,v) + d_v(y)}; susceptible to count-to-infinity

glossary.definition: A set of tuples over a fixed schema (attribute names and domains); the fundamental structure in the relational model.
X → Y holds if any two tuples agreeing on attributes X must agree on attributes Y; basis for normalization theory.
For every non-trivial FD X → Y, X must be a superkey; eliminates all redundancy from FDs; may not preserve all dependencies.
For every non-trivial FD X → A, either X is a superkey or A is prime (part of some key); always achieves lossless join + dependency-preserving decomposition.
Transaction properties: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state → valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions appear serial), Durability (committed changes survive crashes).
A concurrent transaction schedule equivalent to some serial execution of the same transactions; the gold standard for correctness.
Concurrency control protocol: transactions acquire all locks before releasing any (growing phase then shrinking phase); guarantees serializability.
Each write creates a new version; readers access a consistent snapshot without blocking writers; used in PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle.
Variant of B-tree where all data pointers are in leaves (linked for range scans) and internal nodes hold only routing keys; standard database index structure.
The execution strategy chosen by the query optimizer, expressed as a tree of relational operators (scan, join, sort); cost estimated using statistics and cardinality.
A distributed system can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance; formalized by Brewer and proven by Gilbert & Lynch.
Durability mechanism: all changes are logged to stable storage before being applied to the data pages; enables crash recovery and REDO/UNDO.

canon.name: Armstrong's axioms
canon.form: Reflexivity: Y⊆X → X→Y; Augmentation: X→Y → XZ→YZ; Transitivity: X→Y, Y→Z → X→Z; sound and complete for FD closure

canon.name: Relational algebra operators
canon.form: σ (select), π (project), ⋈ (natural join), × (Cartesian product), ∪, ∩, − (set ops), ρ (rename); complete basis for relational queries

canon.name: BCNF decomposition
canon.form: While relation R has FD X→Y violating BCNF: decompose R into R1=(X∪Y) and R2=(R−Y+X); lossless but may not preserve all FDs

canon.name: 2PL → serializability
canon.form: Any schedule produced by strict 2PL (hold locks until commit/abort) is conflict-serializable; the dependency graph is acyclic

canon.name: CAP theorem
canon.form: In an asynchronous network, no distributed system can simultaneously guarantee Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance (proven by impossibility of linearizability under partition)

glossary.definition: The interface between hardware and software defining the set of instructions, registers, addressing modes, data types, and memory model a processor implements.
Reduced Instruction Set Computer: simple, fixed-length instructions, large register file, load-store architecture, hardwired control; examples: MIPS, ARM, RISC-V.
Complex Instruction Set Computer: variable-length instructions, memory operands, complex addressing modes, microcode; example: x86.
Condition preventing the next instruction from executing in its expected clock cycle; types: structural (resource conflict), data (RAW/WAW/WAR dependency), control (branch).
True data dependency: an instruction reads a register before a preceding instruction has written it; resolved by forwarding or stalling.
A hit occurs when requested data is found in cache (fast); a miss requires fetching from a slower memory level; miss types: compulsory, capacity, conflict.
Temporal locality: recently accessed data likely reaccessed soon; spatial locality: data near recently accessed data likely accessed soon; justifies caching.
Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line (index = address mod #lines); simple but prone to conflict misses.
Cache with k lines per set; a block maps to one set, can occupy any of k lines; k-way SA balances hit rate and hardware cost.
AMAT = hit time + miss rate × miss penalty; fundamental metric for cache design.
Processor mechanism to guess the outcome of a branch before it is resolved; misprediction causes pipeline flush (penalty = pipeline depth).
CPU executes instructions as operands become available rather than in program order; uses register renaming (Tomasulo algorithm) to avoid false dependencies.

canon.name: AMAT formula
canon.form: AMAT = L1_hit_time + L1_miss_rate × (L2_hit_time + L2_miss_rate × DRAM_penalty)

canon.name: Amdahl's law
canon.form: Speedup_overall = 1 / ((1 - f) + f/s); f = fraction parallelizable, s = speedup of that fraction; limits parallel scaling

canon.name: Tomasulo algorithm
canon.form: Dynamic scheduling with register renaming using reservation stations; eliminates WAR and WAW hazards; executes instructions out-of-order as operands arrive

canon.name: Pipeline CPI
canon.form: CPI = 1 + stall_cycles_per_instruction; ideal CPI=1 for single-issue pipeline; stalls from hazards and cache misses add fractional cycles

canon.name: Cache size / associativity tradeoff
canon.form: Miss rate generally decreases with size (capacity) and associativity (conflicts); direct-mapped fastest but most conflict misses; fully associative fewest misses but hardware cost O(n)

canon.name: Memory consistency (sequential)
canon.form: Sequential consistency (Lamport): result of any execution is the same as if operations of all processors were executed in some sequential order; stronger than most hardware models

subtopics: Abstract syntax trees and semantic analysis

glossary.definition: Compiler phase converting a character stream into a token stream using regular expressions / finite automata; recognizes identifiers, literals, operators, keywords.
Compiler phase constructing an abstract syntax tree from a token stream; verifies syntactic correctness; based on CFG; outputs an AST.
Top-down parseable grammar using k lookahead tokens; FIRST and FOLLOW sets determine parse table; LL(1) is the most common.
Bottom-up parseable grammar using k lookahead tokens; more powerful than LL(k); variants: SLR, LALR(1) (used in yacc/bison), canonical LR(1).
Tree representation of program structure after parsing; omits syntactic sugar; basis for semantic analysis, type checking, and code generation.
Set of rules assigning types to expressions; a sound type system guarantees no type errors at runtime for well-typed programs (progress + preservation).
Static: types checked at compile time (Java, C, Haskell); dynamic: types checked at runtime (Python, JavaScript); gradual typing mixes both.
IR where each variable is assigned exactly once; uses φ-functions at merge points; simplifies many optimizations (constant propagation, dead code elimination).
Compile-time analysis propagating information along control flow graph edges to determine program properties; examples: live variables, reaching definitions, constant folding.
Mapping of unlimited virtual registers to finite physical registers; modeled as graph coloring (interference graph); NP-complete in general, heuristics used.
Automatic memory management reclaiming unreachable objects; algorithms: reference counting, mark-and-sweep, copying, generational GC.
First-class function paired with its captured environment (free variables); enables higher-order programming; implemented via heap-allocated environment record.

A formal specification of a language's syntactic structure as production rules (e.g., context-free grammar); the basis for parsers. Chomsky hierarchy: regular < context-free < context-sensitive < recursively enumerable.
In object-oriented programming, a class is a blueprint defining the structure (fields) and behavior (methods) of objects; supports encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.

canon.name: FIRST/FOLLOW set construction
canon.form: FIRST(α): terminals that can begin strings derived from α; FOLLOW(A): terminals that can follow A in some sentential form; used to build LL(1) parse tables

canon.name: Shift-reduce parsing (LR)
canon.form: Parser maintains a stack; on each token either shifts (push) or reduces (pop RHS, push LHS); action/goto tables encode grammar; O(n) parse time

canon.name: Type soundness (Progress + Preservation)
canon.form: Progress: a well-typed term is either a value or can take a step; Preservation: if a well-typed term takes a step, the result is well-typed; together imply no stuck states

canon.name: Graph coloring register allocation
canon.form: Build interference graph (nodes=variables, edges=simultaneous live ranges); k-color for k registers; NP-complete; Chaitin's algorithm: simplify, spill, select

canon.name: Reaching definitions dataflow
canon.form: Forward analysis; gen[B] = defs in B not killed later in B; kill[B] = all defs of variables defined in B from other blocks; in[B] = ∪ out[pred]; out[B] = gen[B] ∪ (in[B] − kill[B])

glossary.definition: Test error = bias² + variance + irreducible noise; high bias = underfitting (model too simple); high variance = overfitting (model too complex); regularization and ensembles help.
Iterative optimization: θ ← θ − η∇L(θ); η is the learning rate; variants: SGD (one example), mini-batch, momentum, Adam.
Efficient computation of gradients in a neural network using the chain rule applied backward through the computation graph; O(forward pass) time.
Model memorizes training data, failing to generalize; diagnosed by high train accuracy but high test error; addressed by regularization, dropout, data augmentation, early stopping.
Adding a penalty to the loss to discourage complex models; L2 (ridge): +λ||w||², shrinks weights; L1 (lasso): +λ||w||_1, produces sparsity.
Classifier maximizing the margin between classes; decision boundary defined by support vectors; kernel trick enables nonlinear boundaries implicitly in high-dimensional spaces.
Replaces dot products ⟨x,z⟩ with a kernel function K(x,z) = ⟨φ(x),φ(z)⟩, enabling nonlinear classifiers without explicitly computing high-dimensional feature maps.
Expectation-Maximization: iteratively computes expected log-likelihood (E-step) and maximizes it over parameters (M-step); converges to local maximum; used for GMMs, HMMs.
Probably Approximately Correct: a concept class is PAC-learnable if there is an algorithm that with probability ≥ 1-δ outputs a hypothesis with error ≤ ε using poly(1/ε, 1/δ, n) samples.
Largest set of points that a hypothesis class can shatter (classify in all 2^m ways); higher VC dimension = more expressive but more data needed for generalization.
Computes a weighted sum of value vectors, weights determined by compatibility between query and key vectors; enables Transformers to model long-range dependencies in O(n²) time.
L = −Σ_i y_i log(p_i); standard loss for classification; equivalent to negative log-likelihood under a categorical distribution; minimized by gradient descent.
σ(z)_i = exp(z_i) / Σ_j exp(z_j); maps logit vector to probability distribution; numerically stabilized by subtracting max(z).

canon.name: Perceptron update rule
canon.form: w ← w + y·x if misclassified (y·(w·x) ≤ 0); converges if data is linearly separable (Perceptron Convergence Theorem)

canon.name: Logistic regression gradient
canon.form: L = −Σ[y_i log(σ(w·x_i)) + (1−y_i)log(1−σ(w·x_i))]; ∇_w L = Σ(σ(w·x_i)−y_i)x_i; convex → global minimum

canon.name: Backpropagation chain rule
canon.form: ∂L/∂w = ∂L/∂z · ∂z/∂w; gradients flow backward through computation graph; each layer needs local Jacobian; O(n) in network size

canon.name: SVM primal objective
canon.form: min_{w,b} (1/2)||w||² subject to y_i(w·x_i + b) ≥ 1; soft-margin adds slack: +C Σξ_i; dual: max Σα_i − (1/2)Σα_iα_jy_iy_jK(x_i,x_j)

canon.name: Bias-variance decomposition
canon.form: E[(y−f̂(x))²] = (Bias[f̂(x)])² + Var[f̂(x)] + σ²_noise; bias = E[f̂]−f; variance = E[(f̂−E[f̂])²]

canon.name: VC generalization bound
canon.form: With probability ≥ 1−δ: error(h) ≤ err_train(h) + √((VC(H)·ln(2m/VC(H)) + ln(4/δ)) / (2m)) for m training examples

canon.name: k-means algorithm
canon.form: Initialize k centroids; alternate: assign each point to nearest centroid, recompute centroids as cluster means; converges to local optimum; O(nkd) per iteration

canon.name: PCA via SVD
canon.form: Center data X; compute SVD: X = UΣV^T; principal components = columns of V; project onto top-k: X_k = XV_k; captures maximum variance directions

canon.name: Transformer self-attention
canon.form: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k) · V; Q=XW_Q, K=XW_K, V=XW_V; multi-head: concat h heads, project; O(n²d) per layer

subtopics: Probability axioms, conditional probability, Bayes' theorem
Common distributions (Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Gaussian, Exponential)

glossary.definition: P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B); relates conditional and marginal probabilities; foundation of Bayesian inference.
E[X] = Σ x·P(X=x) (discrete) or ∫x·f(x)dx (continuous); linearity: E[aX+bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] always (no independence required).
Var(X) = E[(X−E[X])²] = E[X²] − (E[X])²; Var(aX+b) = a²Var(X); Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) if X,Y independent.
Sample mean X̄_n → E[X] as n→∞ in probability (weak LLN) or almost surely (strong LLN); basis for Monte Carlo estimation.
√n(X̄_n − μ) / σ → N(0,1) in distribution as n→∞; sample means of i.i.d. finite-variance variables are approximately Gaussian.
Sequence of random variables where P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n, ..., X_0) = P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n); memoryless; characterized by transition matrix P.
Distribution π such that πP = π; for ergodic chains, π is unique and the chain converges to π from any starting state.
θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ Σ log p(x_i; θ); finds parameter maximizing probability of observed data; consistent and asymptotically efficient under regularity conditions.
Probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true; small p-value (< α) → reject H_0.
P(X=k) = λ^k e^{-λ}/k!; models count of rare events in fixed interval; E[X]=Var(X)=λ; Poisson approximates Binomial when n large, p small, λ=np.

A continuous-time counting process whose events occur independently at a constant average rate lambda; inter-arrival times are exponential and the count in any interval of length t is Poisson(lambda*t) -- the continuous limit of Bernoulli trials.

canon.name: Markov's inequality
canon.form: For X ≥ 0: P(X ≥ a) ≤ E[X]/a; weakest but most general concentration bound

canon.name: Chebyshev's inequality
canon.form: P(|X − μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k²; uses variance; P(|X−μ| ≥ t) ≤ Var(X)/t²

canon.name: Chernoff bound
canon.form: For sum X = X_1+...+X_n of independent [0,1] r.v.s with mean μ: P(X ≥ (1+δ)μ) ≤ exp(−μδ²/3) for δ≤1; exponentially tight

canon.name: Law of total expectation
canon.form: E[X] = E[E[X|Y]]; equivalently E[X] = Σ_y E[X|Y=y]·P(Y=y)

canon.name: Bayes' theorem (expanded)
canon.form: P(H|E) = P(E|H)·P(H) / [P(E|H)·P(H) + P(E|¬H)·P(¬H)]; posterior ∝ likelihood × prior

subtopics: Node voltage and mesh current methods

glossary.definition: The sum of voltages around any closed loop in a circuit equals zero; a consequence of energy conservation.
The sum of currents entering any node equals the sum of currents leaving; a consequence of charge conservation.
Any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by a voltage source V_th in series with a resistance R_th; V_th = open-circuit voltage, R_th = resistance with independent sources zeroed.
Any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by a current source I_N in parallel with R_N; I_N = short-circuit current, R_N = R_th; I_N = V_th / R_th.
In a linear circuit with multiple independent sources, the response is the sum of responses due to each source acting alone (others zeroed).
Element storing energy in electric field; V = (1/C)∫i dt; i = C·dV/dt; impedance Z_C = 1/(jωC) in frequency domain; blocks DC, passes AC.
Element storing energy in magnetic field; V = L·di/dt; i = (1/L)∫V dt; impedance Z_L = jωL; blocks AC, passes DC.
RC: τ = RC; RL: τ = L/R; time for exponential response to reach 1−1/e ≈ 63.2% of final value; system response ∝ exp(−t/τ).
Operational amplifier: high-gain differential voltage amplifier; ideal: infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite open-loop gain; used with negative feedback for precise circuits.
Z = R + jX; generalization of resistance to AC circuits; Z_R = R; Z_C = 1/(jωC) = −j/(ωC); Z_L = jωL; Ohm's law: V = IZ in phasor domain.
cos(θ) where θ is the phase angle between voltage and current; PF=1 for purely resistive load; PF<1 for reactive loads; P = V_rms·I_rms·PF.

canon.name: Ohm's law
canon.form: V = I·R; R in ohms, I in amperes, V in volts; power P = VI = I²R = V²/R

canon.name: KVL
canon.form: Σ V_k = 0 around any closed loop (sign convention: voltage rise positive or negative by convention)

canon.name: KCL
canon.form: Σ I_in = Σ I_out at any node; equivalently Σ_k I_k = 0 with consistent sign convention

canon.name: Thevenin theorem
canon.form: V_th = V_oc (open-circuit voltage at terminals); R_th = V_oc / I_sc = resistance seen at terminals with independent sources zeroed

canon.name: RC transient response
canon.form: V_C(t) = V_f + (V_0 − V_f)·e^{−t/(RC)}; V_0 = initial voltage, V_f = final voltage; τ = RC

canon.name: RLC natural frequency
canon.form: ω_0 = 1/√(LC); damping ratio ζ = R/(2)·√(C/L); overdamped ζ>1, critically damped ζ=1, underdamped ζ<1

canon.name: Inverting op-amp gain
canon.form: V_out = −(R_f / R_in)·V_in; gain set by ratio of feedback to input resistor; virtual ground at inverting input

canon.name: Non-inverting op-amp gain
canon.form: V_out = (1 + R_f/R_1)·V_in; input impedance ≈ ∞; output impedance ≈ 0

canon.name: Maximum power transfer
canon.form: Maximum power delivered to load when R_L = R_th (Thevenin resistance); P_max = V_th² / (4·R_th)

glossary.definition: Linear Time-Invariant system: satisfies superposition (linearity) and output doesn't depend on absolute time (time-invariance); fully characterized by its impulse response h(t).
(f * g)(t) = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} f(τ)g(t−τ)dτ; output of LTI system: y(t) = x(t) * h(t); discrete: y[n] = Σ_k x[k]h[n−k].
Output of an LTI system when input is the Dirac delta δ(t); any LTI system output = input convolved with h(t).
X(jω) = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} x(t)e^{−jωt}dt; maps signal from time domain to frequency domain; inverse: x(t) = (1/2π)∫X(jω)e^{jωt}dω.
X(s) = ∫_0^{∞} x(t)e^{−st}dt; generalizes Fourier transform to complex s = σ+jω; converts differential equations to algebraic equations.
H(s) = Y(s)/X(s) for LTI system with zero initial conditions; ratio of Laplace transforms of output and input; encodes poles and zeros.
A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency f_max can be perfectly reconstructed from samples taken at f_s ≥ 2·f_max (Nyquist rate); sampling below causes aliasing.
Distortion when a signal is sampled below the Nyquist rate; high-frequency components appear as lower-frequency imposters; prevented by anti-aliasing (low-pass) filter before sampling.
X(z) = Σ_{n=−∞}^{∞} x[n]z^{−n}; discrete-time analog of Laplace transform; H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) for DT-LTI systems.
X(e^{jω}) = Σ_{n=−∞}^{∞} x[n]e^{−jωn}; frequency representation of discrete-time signals; periodic with period 2π.
Log-frequency plots of magnitude |H(jω)| in dB = 20log10|H(jω)| and phase ∠H(jω) vs log(ω); asymptotic approximations use ±20dB/decade per pole/zero.

canon.name: Convolution theorem
canon.form: Multiplication in frequency domain ↔ convolution in time domain: F{x*h} = X(jω)·H(jω); F{x·h} = (1/2π)X(jω)*H(jω)

canon.name: Fourier transform pairs (key)
canon.form: δ(t) ↔ 1; rect(t) ↔ sinc(ω); e^{−at}u(t) ↔ 1/(a+jω); cos(ω_0 t) ↔ π[δ(ω−ω_0)+δ(ω+ω_0)]; Gaussian ↔ Gaussian

canon.name: Parseval's theorem
canon.form: ∫_{-∞}^{∞}|x(t)|²dt = (1/2π)∫_{-∞}^{∞}|X(jω)|²dω; energy in time domain = energy in frequency domain (up to 1/2π)

canon.name: Laplace transform properties
canon.form: Differentiation: L{x'(t)} = sX(s)−x(0); Integration: L{∫x dt} = X(s)/s; Shift: L{x(t−t_0)u(t−t_0)} = e^{−st_0}X(s)

canon.name: Nyquist sampling theorem
canon.form: x(t) = Σ_n x[n]·sinc((t−nT)/T) if sampled at T ≤ 1/(2f_max); i.e., f_s ≥ 2B for bandwidth B

canon.name: Frequency response of LTI
canon.form: H(jω) = |H(jω)|·e^{j∠H(jω)}; output Y(jω) = H(jω)·X(jω); magnitude response = gain at each frequency; phase response = delay

canon.name: Fourier series
canon.form: x(t) = Σ_{k=−∞}^{∞} c_k e^{jkω_0 t}; c_k = (1/T)∫_T x(t)e^{−jkω_0 t}dt; for periodic x(t) with period T=2π/ω_0

glossary.definition: Control system where the output is not measured or fed back; simpler but sensitive to disturbances and model errors.
Control system where the output is measured and compared to the reference; error drives the controller; reduces sensitivity to disturbances and plant variations.
G(s) = Y(s)/U(s) for a linear system; encodes dynamics as ratio of polynomials in s; poles = roots of denominator, zeros = roots of numerator.
Value of s where transfer function goes to infinity (root of denominator); determines system modes; poles with Re(s) < 0 → stable mode; Re(s) > 0 → unstable.
Bounded-input bounded-output stability: system is BIBO stable iff all poles have negative real parts; equivalently, impulse response is absolutely integrable.
Algebraic test for stability: construct Routh array from characteristic polynomial coefficients; system is stable iff all entries in first column are positive.
Locus of closed-loop poles as gain K varies from 0 to ∞; starts at open-loop poles (K=0), ends at open-loop zeros or infinity; designed for transient response shaping.
Controller with Proportional (K_p·e), Integral (K_i∫e dt), and Derivative (K_d·de/dt) terms; P reduces error, I eliminates steady-state error, D improves transient response.
How much open-loop gain can increase before instability; GM = 1/|G(jω_pc)| in linear scale, where ω_pc is phase crossover frequency (∠G = -180°).
Additional phase lag at gain crossover frequency (|G(jω_gc)|=1) before instability; PM = 180° + ∠G(jω_gc); good design: GM > 6 dB, PM > 30-45°.
Error as t→∞ for a given reference input; e_ss = lim_{s→0} s·E(s) by Final Value Theorem; depends on system type (number of pure integrators in open-loop).

canon.name: Closed-loop transfer function
canon.form: T(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s)H(s)); forward path G(s), feedback H(s); sensitivity S = 1/(1+GH); complement T = GH/(1+GH)

canon.name: Routh-Hurwitz stability test
canon.form: Form Routh table from coefficients of char. polynomial a_n s^n + ... + a_0; system stable iff all entries in column 1 positive; # sign changes = # RHP poles

canon.name: Final value theorem
canon.form: lim_{t→∞} f(t) = lim_{s→0} s·F(s); valid if limit exists and sF(s) has no poles on imaginary axis or RHP

canon.name: Initial value theorem
canon.form: lim_{t→0+} f(t) = lim_{s→∞} s·F(s); valid if f(t) has no impulses at t=0

canon.name: PID transfer function
canon.form: C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d·s = (K_d s² + K_p s + K_i)/s; adds zero(s) to root locus to improve stability and response

canon.name: State-space representation
canon.form: ẋ = Ax + Bu; y = Cx + Du; A: state matrix, B: input matrix, C: output matrix, D: feedthrough; eigenvalues of A = system poles

canon.name: Nyquist stability criterion
canon.form: Z = N + P; Z = # closed-loop RHP poles, N = # clockwise encirclements of −1 by Nyquist plot, P = # open-loop RHP poles; stable iff Z = 0

glossary.definition: Algebraic structure with two elements {0,1}, operations AND (·), OR (+), NOT (¬); satisfies commutativity, associativity, distributivity, identity, complement, and absorption laws.
A product term in which every variable appears exactly once (complemented or not); minterm m_i corresponds to the binary representation of i; SOP form = sum of minterms.
A sum term in which every variable appears exactly once; POS form = product of maxterms; maxterm M_i corresponds to minterms with the same index.
Boolean expression as OR of AND terms; canonical SOP = sum of all minterms where function = 1; can be implemented with two-level AND-OR logic.
Grid-based minimization tool for Boolean functions; adjacent cells differ by one variable; groupings of 1, 2, 4, 8... cells give prime implicants.
Logic circuit whose outputs depend only on current inputs, not history; no feedback or state; implemented with gates; examples: adder, MUX, decoder.
Logic circuit with memory/state; outputs depend on inputs AND current state; implemented with flip-flops; examples: counters, registers, FSMs.
Edge-triggered storage element; Q follows D on rising clock edge; Q+ = D; used for registers and pipelines.
FSM where outputs depend on both current state and current inputs; output can change mid-state; generally fewer states than equivalent Moore machine.
FSM where outputs depend only on current state; outputs change only on state transitions; more predictable, used in most hardware FSM designs.
Input combination that never occurs or for which output is irrelevant; can be assigned 0 or 1 to simplify Boolean expression; marked X in K-maps.
Momentary incorrect output in combinational logic due to different propagation delays along paths; static hazards (brief wrong constant), dynamic hazards (multiple transitions).
A data type with two values (true/false or 1/0); Boolean operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) are the basis of digital logic and circuit design.

canon.name: De Morgan's theorem
canon.form: NOT(A AND B) = NOT(A) OR NOT(B); NOT(A OR B) = NOT(A) AND NOT(B); enables NAND/NOR universal gate implementation

canon.name: Boolean identities
canon.form: A+A=A, A·A=A (idempotency); A+1=1, A·0=0 (annihilation); A+0=A, A·1=A (identity); A+A'=1, A·A'=0 (complement); A''=A (double negation)

canon.name: Full adder
canon.form: Sum = A XOR B XOR C_in; Carry_out = (A AND B) OR (C_in AND (A XOR B)); 2-level logic; ripple-carry adder chains n full adders; O(n) delay

canon.name: Ripple-carry vs carry-lookahead
canon.form: Ripple: O(n) delay, O(n) area; Carry-lookahead: G_i = A_i·B_i, P_i = A_i+B_i; C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i·C_i; O(log n) delay with O(n log n) area

canon.name: Shannon expansion (cofactor)
canon.form: f(x_1,...,x_n) = x_i · f_{x_i=1} + x_i' · f_{x_i=0}; basis for BDD-based CAD tools and multiplexer implementations

canon.name: Quine-McCluskey algorithm
canon.form: Systematic tabular method to find all prime implicants and select minimum cover; equivalent to K-map minimization but algorithmic and scalable

glossary.definition: Interface between p-type and n-type semiconductor; built-in potential V_bi = (kT/q)·ln(N_A·N_D/n_i²); under forward bias, current flows exponentially; reverse bias, small leakage.
I = I_0(e^{V/(nV_T)} − 1); I_0 = reverse saturation current; n = ideality factor (1-2); V_T = kT/q ≈ 26 mV at room temperature.
Three-terminal device (Base, Collector, Emitter); in active mode: I_C = β·I_B; I_E = (1+β)·I_B; controlled current amplifier; β (h_FE) typically 50-300.

Metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistor; gate voltage controls channel; n-channel NMOS: I_D = μ_n C_ox (W/L)[(V_GS−V_th)V_DS − V_DS²/2] (triode); I_D = (μ_n C_ox W/2L)(V_GS−V_th)² (saturation).
Gate voltage at which MOSFET channel inverts and current begins to flow; depends on oxide thickness, doping, and substrate bias.
Logic family using pairs of NMOS and PMOS transistors; NMOS pulls output low, PMOS pulls output high; no DC current in steady state → low static power; standard for digital ICs.
NM_H = V_OH − V_IH; NM_L = V_IL − V_OL; measure of robustness against noise; CMOS: NM ≈ V_DD/2 for symmetric gates.
g_m = ∂I_D/∂V_GS = 2I_D/(V_GS−V_th) for MOSFET in saturation; g_m = I_C/V_T for BJT; key small-signal parameter relating input voltage to output current.
Linear approximation of transistor behavior around a DC operating point (Q-point); MOSFET: controlled current source g_m·v_gs with output resistance r_o = |V_A|/I_D; valid for small signal swings.
P_dynamic = α·C·V_DD²·f; α = activity factor (0-1), C = load capacitance, V_DD = supply voltage, f = clock frequency; dominant power consumption in digital ICs.

canon.name: Diode Shockley equation
canon.form: I = I_S(e^{V_D/(nV_T)} - 1); V_T = kT/q = 25.85 mV at 300K; forward voltage drop ≈ 0.6-0.7V for Si at room temperature

canon.name: MOSFET drain current (NMOS)
canon.form: Triode: I_D = μ_n C_ox (W/L)[(V_GS−V_th)V_DS − V_DS²/2]; Saturation (V_DS ≥ V_GS−V_th): I_D = (μ_n C_ox W/2L)(V_GS−V_th)²(1 + λV_DS)

canon.name: BJT current relationships
canon.form: I_C = β·I_B = α·I_E; I_E = I_B + I_C; α = β/(1+β); in active mode: V_BE ≈ 0.7V (Si); I_C = I_S·e^{V_BE/V_T}

canon.name: CMOS inverter switching threshold
canon.form: V_M ≈ V_DD/2 for matched CMOS (equal drive strength); gate switches at midpoint; noise margins NM_H = NM_L ≈ V_DD/2 ideally

canon.name: CMOS dynamic power
canon.form: P = α C_L V_DD² f; energy per switching event = C_L V_DD²; static leakage P_static = I_leak · V_DD becoming significant below 65nm

canon.name: Miller effect
canon.form: Input capacitance C_in = C_gd(1 + g_m R_L); feedback capacitance C_gd is amplified by (1+gain) at input; limits high-frequency response of amplifiers
